title: Chapter Twenty: The Second Proposal
content: It started in my legs.

Not in my chest, not in my head — in the meat and bone of my thighs, the tendons behind my knees. A pulling sensation, like hands wrapped around my muscles from the inside, squeezing and releasing in a rhythm that was not my own. I was sitting at my desk, pen in hand, mid-sentence in my journal, when my left leg extended beneath me. Then my right. I stood without deciding to stand.
The pen clattered to the floor.

I knew what it was before I understood it. My body knew before my mind caught up. That cold certainty that had lived in Blackwood Vale for three hundred years — the curse reaching for me the way it had reached for every Aurelia before, the way tide reaches shore. Not asking. Taking.
Julian was proposing again.

Not with flowers. Not with a painting of my face rendered in stolen light. This time he was using the full weight of the curse itself, pressing down on me like a hand on the back of my neck, and it was not a request. It was a compulsion. The fog outside my window thickened as I watched — not drifting, not curling, but *massing*, gathering itself like a fist. And my legs walked me toward the door.
No.

I gripped the doorframe. My fingers white-knuckled against the wood, and for one blinding moment I held. My legs strained. My hips tilted forward. The pull was so strong I felt my shoulder joints stretch, felt my fingertips begin to slip, and all the while my lips were moving without sound, shaping the word *yes* over and over like a prayer I refused to voice.
My right hand let go.
Then my left.

I was walking. Down the stairs, through the front hall, out the front door. The fog swallowed me. It was dense — denser than I'd ever felt it, denser than the morning Hazel had appeared to me in the graveyard. It pressed against my skin, cold and wet and *eager*, like something alive that had been waiting for this moment. I could taste it on my tongue, mineral and old, the flavor of deep earth and sealed crypts. My feet found the cobblestones without my guidance. Left. Right. Left. The route to Julian's studio was carved into my muscles the way a river carves a valley — three hundred years of Aurelias walking this same path, their bodies not their own, their mouths forming words they didn't choose.

I was screaming inside. A soundless, thrashing scream that bounced around the hollow of my skull and went nowhere. My body moved with mechanical precision. My arms swung at my sides. My chin lifted. I could feel the word *yes* sitting in my throat like a stone, like bile, rising and rising, and I clenched my teeth so hard my jaw cracked with pain — but the word kept rising. It was at the back of my tongue now. It was pressing against my teeth.
*Yes.*

The studio loomed. I could see the warm amber light through the frost-laced window, could see the silhouette of a man standing perfectly still, waiting. He'd been waiting. He'd known this was coming. Maybe he'd triggered it himself — maybe I'd done something, learned something, *won* something that pushed him to this. The curse was not patient anymore. It was desperate. Something had changed, and Julian Cross knew he was losing.
My hand reached for the gate.
And I found Hazel's gift.

It was there — in the space between one heartbeat and the next, the space where the Illusion Weaving lived. I'd practiced it. I'd *used* it, in the graveyard, in the archives, in small secret ways that no one had seen. Hazel had given it to me, and it had been growing stronger every day, like a muscle I didn't know I had. Now I seized it with everything I had.

The pulling in my legs didn't stop. The fog didn't thin. The word *yes* was still clawing at my teeth. But I split. Or — I split *from myself*. I felt something tear free from my body, painless and precise, the way you peel a sticker from its backing. I was still walking — the curse still had my legs, my arms, my spine — but I was also standing still. I was two Aurelias. One real, one woven from light and memory and the cunning of a dead woman.

The illusion was perfect. It had my face, my hunched shoulders, my panicked eyes. It walked through the gate I'd been reaching for, opened the studio door, stepped inside. I watched from the fog, pressed against the garden wall, invisible, my real body finally slumping as the curse's pull redirected itself toward the decoy. The compulsion was so strong it couldn't tell the difference. It was hungry, not discerning. It wanted my consent, and here was my shape, my voice, my submission — close enough.
I heard my own voice say *yes*.

It came from inside the studio, warm and immediate, and it was my voice but not my voice — it was the voice of the illusion, crafted so precisely that even I felt a chill of recognition. The decoy said everything Julian wanted to hear. *I've been fighting it. I'm tired of fighting. Yes.* The words fell into the amber-lit room like coins into a well.
Julian accepted it.

I watched through the studio window, my breath shallow, my body trembling with the aftershock of possession. He stepped toward the illusion — toward *me*, the me he believed was real — and his face was soft. Open. His hand reached out and brushed the decoy's cheek, and the illusion held, warm and solid beneath his fingers. He smiled, and it was the smile that had haunted me for months, the beautiful lie I'd almost believed in the beginning. He leaned in.
"Finally," he whispered, and I heard it through the glass, clear as church bells.
The illusion said his name. It touched his chest. It moved its lips in the shape of surrender.
And Julian Cross was happy.
For about thirty seconds.

I saw the moment it happened. His hand was still on the decoy's cheek, and his expression was still soft, and then — something shifted. A flicker behind his eyes. His fingers pressed *in*, and I knew he was feeling for a pulse that wasn't there, warmth that wasn't quite right, the subtle wrongness of a thing woven from light instead of born from blood. The confusion came first. His brow creased. His hand stilled. Then recognition — a slow, sickening dawn, the way you realize you've been standing on thin ice only when it begins to crack.
Then rage.
"You think you can *trick* me?"

His voice was different. Stripped. The charm was gone, the warmth, the careful sculpting of every word and gesture. What was left was raw and *hungry* — a sound like stone grinding against stone, like something ancient and furious that had been wearing a man's face. The studio light seemed to flinch. The illusion flickered — I felt the strain of it, the effort of maintaining the weave from across the garden — but it held. Julian's beautiful face contorted into something terrible, lips peeled back from his teeth, his hand closing into a fist.
"Aurelia."

He said my name and it wasn't a lover's murmur. It was a curse of its own. He looked directly at the window — directly at *me*, it felt like, though I was invisible, though the fog should have hidden me — and for one frozen moment I saw the real Julian Cross. Not the handsome artist, not the grieving almost-widower, not the charming phantom who'd been courting me with paintings and sorrow. This was the predator underneath. Hungry. Desperate. *Angrier than I'd ever seen him* because his prey had learned to fight.

He lunged at the illusion. His hand passed through it — through the image of my face, my shoulders, my body — and the decoy scattered like morning mist. The weave came apart in ribbons of fading light, and I felt it snap back through me, a psychic whiplash that made my vision swim.

Julian stood in the empty space where I'd been. His chest was heaving. His fists were clenched at his sides. The studio behind him was wrecked — canvases slashed, paint tubes crushed under his feet, the remnants of whatever false tenderness he'd prepared for this moment scattered and ruined. He didn't look at any of it. He looked at the window, at the fog, at the darkness that held me.
"You think you can *trick* me?" he said again, quieter this time, and the quiet was worse than the shouting. "You think your little ghost tricks will save you?"

But it wasn't just Julian. That was the thing I understood as I pressed myself against the garden wall, my heart slamming so hard I could feel it in my throat, my fingertips, the soles of my feet. The rage pouring out of the studio wasn't his alone. It was the curse's rage. The fury of a thing that had fed on Blackwood Vale for three centuries and now found its latest meal *biting back*. Julian was its vessel, but the anger was older than him, larger than him, larger than the studio, larger than the street—
The fog thickened.

I mean that literally. It went from dense to *solid*, from something that obscured vision to something that resisted movement. I pushed against it and it pushed back, cold and wet and *dense*, like trying to walk through wet concrete. The air itself was becoming an enemy. I could feel it pressing against my chest, making each breath a labor, filling my mouth with the taste of damp rot and old stone. The fog was no longer atmosphere. It was architecture. It was *building something* around me.

At the edge of my vision, shapes flickered. The Hollow Council. I couldn't see them clearly — I never could — but I felt them, the way you feel eyes on the back of your neck. Watching. Not interfering. Not yet. Just... present. Like vultures on a high branch, waiting to see how the hunt played out.
This wasn't over. This wasn't even the real battle.
I ran.

I don't know how I moved through the solid fog. Instinct, maybe. Desperation. The same animal panic that makes prey bolt from a predator even when the predator is faster. My legs were mine again — the curse's pull had broken when the illusion shattered, or maybe it was regrouping, gathering itself for a stronger assault. I didn't wait to find out. I ran through streets that shouldn't have been familiar in fog this thick but were, my feet knowing the way home the way my grandmother's feet had known it, the way every Aurelia's feet had known it, because Blackwood Vale was ours before it was the curse's and the land remembered even when the fog didn't let me see.

I slammed through my front door. Locked it. Pressed my back against it and slid to the floor, gasping, my lungs burning with the effort of breathing fog-thick air, my hands shaking so badly I could barely grip my own knees.
It took me twenty minutes to stop trembling.
It took me another ten before I could pick up my pen.
I fed the curse a ghost and it believed me.
For now.

The illusion held long enough. Long enough for Julian to accept it, long enough for his mask to crack, long enough for me to see what was underneath. I know what he is now. Not charming. Not sorrowful. Not even particularly patient. He's hungry, and he's angry, and he's been wearing a skin suit of fake tenderness because that's what worked before. That's what caught the other Aurelias. A beautiful face. A gentle touch. A whispered *I need you* that felt like love but was a lure.

None of that anymore. The mask isn't cracked. It's *gone*. I took it off him by showing him I could fight, and now there's just the predator, plain and desperate and more dangerous for having nothing left to hide behind.
But here's the part I can't stop thinking about.
You can't fool the fog forever.

The decoy worked because the curse was *eager*. Because it wanted my consent so badly that it took the first crude approximation and called it real. It was hungry, not careful. But it won't be hungry and careless the same way twice. Next time — and there will be a next time, I can feel it gathering already, the fog retreating but not dispersing, just pulling back like a wave before the next crash — next time it will *check*. It will reach for my pulse. It will feel for the warmth of blood. It will know the difference between a woman and a wish.
And the curse doesn't actually need my consent.

That's the part that's keeping me awake. That's the part my hands won't stop shaking long enough to write steadily. It *wanted* my consent — the Aurelias have always said yes, that's how the curse feeds, that's the mechanism, three hundred years of women walking into that studio of their own volition, manipulated but still choosing, still speaking the word that seals the bond. But wanting my consent and *needing* it are two different things. What happened tonight proved that. The curse grabbed my body and *moved* it. It pulled my legs, it shaped my lips, it walked me to that gate without my permission. The only reason it stopped at the gate and not at the altar was because I gave it a decoy to swallow instead.
What if next time there's no gate? What if next time it doesn't ask at all?
It just needs my silence.

One moment of compliance. One instant where I stop fighting, stop splitting, stop weaving. One second where the word *yes* makes it past my teeth and it won't matter whether I meant it or not. The curse is a legalist — it operates on technicalities, on the letter of a three-hundred-year-old law. It doesn't need my heart. It needs my voice. And voices, unlike hearts, can be *made* to speak.
I have a limited supply of decoys.

Hazel's Illusion Weaving is powerful, but it takes something out of me each time. I felt it tonight — the tearing, the splitting, the way the weave snapped back when the illusion broke. I can do it again. Maybe twice more before I'm too empty to weave anything at all. And then it's just me and the fog and a predator who's done pretending.

I outsmarted him tonight. Let me write that down, because it's true and because I might need to remember it later, when the fear gets too thick to think through. I outsmarted Julian Cross. I took an ancestress's gift and turned the curse's own hunger against it. I made it eat a ghost and it *believed* me. That's a victory. That's a genuine, real victory, and I'm allowed to feel it.
But the fog outside my window isn't thinning.
It's waiting.
